edges.notes: Moved from implemented_by.json during Agent 6 review (S3-001): data_splitting is a specific type/variant of resampling, not a concrete algorithmic realization of an abstract concept.
Bayesian inference is an instance of the statistical inference framework.
A linear trend is a specific manifestation of a linear relationship over time or ordered data.

Target may be too broad; confidence interval construction technically relies on the sampling distribution of the estimator. A more precise target might be estimator or standard_error. Confidence lowered to 0.75 pending expert review.

source.canonical_name: Large Sample Confidence Interval using MLE
Large Sample Confidence Interval using MLE

edges.notes: Cook's distance combines residual magnitude and leverage; removing leverage makes the concept incomplete.

Marginal distributions are derived by summing/integrating a joint distribution; without a joint distribution the marginal cannot be defined. Confidence slightly below 1.0 because the target is the discrete case only; a parallel edge to joint_probability_density_function could be added for completeness.

Semantic boundary is ambiguous: the expected value of a linear combination uses expected_value as a concept but this resembles a uses/applies relationship rather than a hard logical dependency. Confidence capped at 0.80 pending review.

Added during Agent 6 review (S4-002): the Wald test statistic is constructed using the Fisher information (or its estimate) to compute the standard error of the MLE. Logical dependency on Fisher information.

Added during Agent 6 review (S4-003): marginal distribution of continuous variables is derived by integrating out the joint PDF. Complements the existing marginal_distribution -> joint_probability_mass_function edge for the discrete case.

Moved from assumes.json during Agent 6 review (S3-001): normality_of_errors is constitutive of the normal_error_model — without this assumption the model is undefined and degenerates into a plain linear regression model. This is a hard logical dependency (requires), not a conditional assumption.

Added during graph construction (isolated node fix): defining a parameter requires a parameter space over which it is defined — the set of all admissible values. Without a parameter space the parameter has no domain of definition.
OLS model is defined over the error term; the regression equation y = Xβ + ε requires ε.
A linear regression model requires an intercept term (unless explicitly suppressed).
Linear regression requires slope coefficient(s) to define the linear relationship.
OLS estimation and inference depend on the variance of the error term σ².
GLM is defined as having a linear predictor η = Xβ; without it GLM cannot be defined.
GLM requires a link function g connecting the linear predictor to the mean: μ = g⁻¹(η).
GLM is defined by three components; the random component specifying the response distribution is one of them.
The systematic component (linear predictor part) is a defining element of the GLM framework.
Linear regression requires a dependent variable (response) to be defined.
Linear regression requires at least one independent variable (predictor).
The posterior distribution is a conditional distribution P(θ|data); its definition requires the concept of conditional distribution.
Bayesian inference is grounded in conditional probability: P(θ|data) ∝ P(data|θ)P(θ).
Independence of continuous random variables is defined in terms of factorisation of the joint PDF.
MLE estimates a parameter; the concept of a parameter is a logical prerequisite for defining MLE.
Decision rule defines the rejection region: reject H₀ when test statistic falls in rejection region.
Hypothesis testing requires a decision rule to map test statistics to reject/fail-to-reject outcomes.
Classical hypothesis testing is defined in terms of a critical value that demarcates the rejection region.
Polynomial regression often uses orthogonal polynomial transformation to reduce collinearity.
Frequentist inference is grounded in the concept of sampling distributions and repeated sampling.
Linear regression assumes a linear relationship between predictors and response.
Logistic regression is defined by the logit link function connecting linear predictor to probability.
The logit link is the inverse of the logistic function; its definition depends on the logistic function.
The PDF is defined as the derivative of the CDF; the CDF is the more fundamental concept.
The PMF and CDF are related: the CDF is the cumulative sum of the PMF; CDF is the foundational representation.
Chebyshev's inequality is stated in terms of variance: P(|X-μ| ≥ kσ) ≤ 1/k². Requires variance.
Chebyshev's inequality is centered around the mean (expected value).
A confidence interval is defined at a specified confidence level (1-α); the concept requires confidence_level.
Hypothesis testing is performed at a specified significance level α; the framework requires this concept.
An interaction regression model is defined by the presence of interaction effects between predictors.
All subsets regression exhaustively searches across models of varying complexity.

Small-sample t CI for a population mean assumes the population is normally distributed. Note: normality_of_errors (Regression and Linear Models domain) is used here as the closest available proxy for population normality, as no dedicated 'population normality' node exists in the domain list. The actual assumption is that the population from which observations are drawn is normally distributed, which is conceptually distinct from regression residual normality.

target.canonical_name: Independent and Identically Distributed Random Variables

source.canonical_name: Large Sample Distribution of the MLE

edges.notes: The one-sample t-test assumes normality of the population from which observations are drawn. Note: normality_of_errors (Regression and Linear Models domain) is used here as the closest available proxy for population normality, as no dedicated 'population normality' node exists in the domain list. The actual assumption is that the population from which observations are drawn is normally distributed, which is conceptually distinct from regression residual normality.

The two-sample t-test assumes normality of each population being compared. Note: normality_of_errors (Regression and Linear Models domain) is used here as the closest available proxy for population normality, as no dedicated 'population normality' node exists in the domain list. The actual assumption is that the population from which observations are drawn is normally distributed, which is conceptually distinct from regression residual normality.

The chi-square test for population variance requires that data are drawn from a normal population; the test statistic follows chi-square exactly only under normality. Note: normality_of_errors (Regression and Linear Models domain) is used here as the closest available proxy for population normality, as no dedicated 'population normality' node exists in the domain list. The actual assumption is that the population from which observations are drawn is normally distributed, which is conceptually distinct from regression residual normality.

Inference using the Pearson correlation coefficient assumes bivariate normality of (X, Y), not regression residual normality. Note: normality_of_errors (Regression and Linear Models domain) is used here as the closest available proxy for population normality, as no dedicated 'population normality' node exists in the domain list. The actual assumption is that the population from which observations are drawn is normally distributed, which is conceptually distinct from regression residual normality.

target.canonical_name: Independent and Identically Distributed Random Variables
Independent and Identically Distributed Random Variables

edges.notes: Z-test can be applied under large-sample CLT approximation without strict normality; small-sample validity requires population normality. Note: normality_of_errors (Regression and Linear Models domain) is used here as the closest available proxy for population normality, as no dedicated 'population normality' node exists in the domain list. The actual assumption is that the population from which observations are drawn is normally distributed, which is conceptually distinct from regression residual normality.

target.canonical_name: Independent and Identically Distributed Random Variables

edges.notes: Normal approximation via CLT requires i.i.d. in the classical setting; still executable without i.i.d. but asymptotic guarantee is weakened.

Ridge regression inherits the linear model structure and its frequentist optimality is studied under Gauss-Markov assumptions including homoscedasticity. However, ridge can also be motivated as a Bayesian estimator under a normal prior, in which case homoscedasticity is not strictly required for point estimation. Confidence 0.88 reflects this dual interpretation: homoscedasticity affects the frequentist optimality of the shrinkage estimator but is not required for the Bayesian motivation.

source.canonical_name: Confidence Interval for Difference of Means

edges.notes: Small-sample CI for difference of means relies on normality of each population; large-sample version relaxes this via CLT. Note: normality_of_errors (Regression and Linear Models domain) is used here as the closest available proxy for population normality, as no dedicated 'population normality' node exists in the domain list. The actual assumption is that the population from which observations are drawn is normally distributed, which is conceptually distinct from regression residual normality.

source.canonical_name: Confidence Interval for Difference of Means

edges.notes: Cross-validation estimates generalization error under the assumption that data are exchangeable/i.i.d.; with dependent data (time series) standard k-fold is not valid.
Same reasoning as k-fold; LOO-CV assumes exchangeability of observations.

Added during Agent 6 review (S4-002): the Wald CI is derived from the asymptotic normality of the MLE, which holds only under regularity conditions. Analogous to wald_test assumes regularity_conditions.

source.canonical_name: Large Sample Confidence Interval using MLE

edges.notes: Added during Agent 6 review (S4-003): this CI method relies on the asymptotic normality of the MLE (large_sample_distribution_of_the_mle), which itself assumes regularity_conditions. Makes the dependency explicit at the method level.

source.canonical_name: Confidence Interval for Difference of Means

edges.notes: Asymptotic distribution: under the null hypothesis and regularity conditions, the LRT statistic (-2 log lambda) converges in distribution to chi-square with degrees of freedom equal to the difference in parameter dimensions (Wilks theorem). The chi-square approximation is exact only asymptotically.

source.canonical_name: Large Sample Distribution of the MLE
Large Sample Confidence Interval using MLE

edges.notes: The parametric bootstrap requires a parametric distributional assumption; the normal distribution is the most common choice but the actual distribution is model-dependent. Edge represents the prototypical case only, not a universal relationship. Confidence kept below 0.85 pending expert confirmation.

Source is a Concept rather than a Method or Model; retained because the t-distribution is intrinsic to the definition of the studentized residual — the ratio that defines it follows a t-distribution under normality of errors, making this a definitional rather than procedural dependency.
VIF is specifically designed to quantify the severity of multicollinearity among predictors.
Tolerance = 1/VIF; it measures multicollinearity just as VIF does.

Inspecting the correlation matrix is a standard diagnostic for detecting pairwise multicollinearity among predictors. Note: correlation matrix inspection only captures bivariate linear relationships; VIF is more sensitive for detecting multicollinearity in the presence of multiple correlated predictors simultaneously.

Cook's Distance measures influence, which combines both outlier (residual) and leverage components; using outlier as target is a simplification. A more precise target would be an influence-point concept, but no such node exists in the domain list. Confidence lowered to 0.80 to reflect this approximation. Agent 6 review (S3-003).
The diagonal elements of the hat matrix directly define the leverage values for each observation.
Leverage diagnostic tools are used to identify and quantify high-leverage observations.
Residual plots are used to visually assess the homoscedasticity assumption of regression models.
Q-Q residual plots are used to assess the normality of error terms.
Standardized residuals are used to identify outlying observations in regression.
Studentized residuals provide a more precise outlier detection tool than standardized residuals.

R-squared is the standard measure of goodness of fit for regression models. Note: r_squared (Model Evaluation and Selection domain) and coefficient_of_determination (Regression and Linear Models domain) represent the same statistical concept in different domain contexts; both measures edges to goodness_of_fit are intentionally retained to capture cross-domain coverage.
Adjusted R-squared measures goodness of fit while penalizing for the number of predictors.
In-sample MSE measures training-set fit quality.
Expected out-of-sample MSE is a direct measure of generalization error.
In GLMs, deviance measures the discrepancy between the fitted model and the saturated model.

AIC is an information criterion that estimates relative prediction error (via Kullback-Leibler divergence); it balances fit against complexity but its primary purpose is to estimate out-of-sample prediction error, not to quantify model complexity as such. Target corrected from model_complexity to prediction_error during Agent 6 review (S3-001).

BIC is a penalized likelihood criterion used for model selection; like AIC it estimates prediction error (with a stronger complexity penalty based on sample size). Target corrected from model_complexity to prediction_error during Agent 6 review (S3-002). Confidence set to 0.85.
Mallows' Cp estimates prediction error and is used for variable selection.
Bias quantifies the systematic deviation of an estimator from the true parameter value.
Standard error quantifies the sampling variability of an estimator.
Sample variance is the standard estimator and measure of population variance.
Sample mean is used to estimate and measure the population mean.
Pearson correlation coefficient quantifies linear correlation between two variables.

The coefficient of determination (R²) measures regression goodness of fit. Note: coefficient_of_determination (Regression and Linear Models domain) and r_squared (Model Evaluation and Selection domain) represent the same statistical concept in different domain contexts; both measures edges to goodness_of_fit are intentionally retained to capture cross-domain coverage.
Residual variance is the estimator of the error term variance in regression.

Added during Agent 6 review (S4-001): pairwise correlation between predictors is a fundamental diagnostic for multicollinearity. Complements correlation_matrix_inspection (index 2) at the individual predictor-pair level.

Added during Agent 6 review (S4-002): the overall F statistic in regression tests whether the model explains a significant portion of variance, directly assessing goodness of fit. Confidence 0.88 as F-statistic is primarily a test statistic rather than a standalone fit measure.

Moved from corresponds_to.json during Agent 6 review (S3-002): generalization_error quantifies and diagnoses overfitting — a measures relationship. Overfitting causes elevated generalization error; generalization_error is the diagnostic quantity that detects it.

Added during graph construction (isolated node fix): consistency is a large-sample property that measures estimator quality — an estimator is consistent if it converges in probability to the true parameter as n grows. Parallel to bias measures estimator.
Stepwise selection navigates model complexity by iteratively adding/removing predictors based on fit criteria.
Mallows' Cp penalizes model complexity and is used to compare models in subset selection.
OLS fitting directly produces fitted (predicted) values for each observation.
OLS fitting produces residuals (observed minus fitted values) as a direct output.
The core output of OLS estimation is the vector of regression coefficient estimates.
OLS minimizes RSS, making RSS a direct output of the estimation procedure.
The MLE method outputs the maximum likelihood estimator as its direct result.

Bootstrap resampling produces the bootstrapped sampling distribution. Confidence lowered to 0.75 as non_parametric_bootstrap (index 9) is the more specific source for this relationship; this edge represents the general bootstrap concept producing the distribution indirectly via its variants.
The bootstrap percentile method produces a confidence interval from bootstrap quantiles.
Non-parametric bootstrap resampling produces an empirical bootstrapped sampling distribution.
Residual analysis produces residual plots as its primary diagnostic output.
Under normality of errors assumptions, OLS produces estimators with a known distribution.
Dummy encoding produces dummy (binary indicator) variables from categorical predictors.
Dummy variable encoding produces the columns of the design matrix for categorical predictors.
The method of moments produces parameter estimators by equating sample and population moments.
Least squares estimation produces parameter estimators by minimizing squared residuals.
Bayesian point estimation methods (posterior mean, median, MAP) produce Bayesian point estimates.
The equal-tail credible interval method produces a credible interval from posterior quantiles.
The HPD interval method produces the highest posterior density credible interval.
K-fold cross validation produces an estimate of generalization error.
LOOCV produces an estimate of generalization error using n-1 training folds.
Logistic regression produces fitted probability values for each observation.
Logistic regression produces deviance as a goodness-of-fit statistic.
Poisson regression produces fitted count values for each observation.
Poisson regression produces deviance as a model fit measure.
Added during Agent 6 review (S4-001): ridge regression produces shrinkage coefficient estimates as its primary output, analogous to OLS producing coefficients.
Added during Agent 6 review (S4-002): logistic regression produces log-odds coefficient estimates in addition to fitted values and deviance.

Moved from corresponds_to.json during Agent 6 review (S3-003): marginal_distribution is obtained by integrating the joint PDF over the other variable(s) — a directional, non-invertible operation. This is a produces relationship, not a symmetric duality.
Centering variables transforms predictor columns, producing a centered design matrix.
Residual analysis produces scatterplots of residuals vs fitted values or predictors.
Direction: alternative_hypothesis (a) < null_hypothesis (n) alphabetically.

Confidence reduced to 0.80 during Agent 6 review (S3-006): bayesian_p_value and credible_interval serve analogous but not strictly dual roles in Bayesian inference (scalar tail probability vs. region estimate). This is an analogical rather than strict structural duality.
Direction: population_mean (po) < sample_mean (sa) alphabetically.
Direction: population_variance (po) < sample_variance (sa) alphabetically.
Direction: expected_out_of_sample_mse (e) < in_sample_mse (i) alphabetically.
Direction: backward_stepwise_selection (b) < forward_stepwise_selection (f) alphabetically.
Direction: interval_estimation (i) < point_estimation (p) alphabetically.
Direction: bayesian_inference (b) < frequentist_inference (f) alphabetically.

Expected value and variance are the first and second central moments, forming the canonical location-scale pair that characterizes any distribution up to second order. Together they are sufficient to characterize normal distributions completely, constituting an analytic duality. This duality is weaker than classical test/CI duality; confidence 0.90 reflects this. Per schema Part H: flagged as a borderline corresponds_to pair requiring explicit justification.
Direction corrected during Agent 6 review (S3-004): continuous_random_variables (c) < discrete_random_variables (d) alphabetically, so source = continuous_random_variables.
Direction corrected during Agent 6 review (S3-005): composite_hypothesis (c) < simple_hypothesis (s) alphabetically, so source = composite_hypothesis.
Direction: one_sided_test (o) < two_sided_test (t) alphabetically. These are symmetric alternative structures for hypothesis tests.

Direction: equal_tail_interval (e) < highest_posterior_density_interval (h) alphabetically. These are the two canonical forms of Bayesian credible intervals — symmetric alternatives for summarizing the posterior.
Direction: akaike_information_criterion (a) < bayesian_information_criterion (b) alphabetically. AIC and BIC are structurally symmetric penalized likelihood criteria differing in penalty magnitude.
Direction: k_fold_cross_validation (k) < leave_one_out_cross_validation (l) alphabetically. LOOCV is the limiting case of K-fold (K=n); they are structurally dual validation strategies.
Direction: non_parametric_bootstrap (n) < parametric_bootstrap (p) alphabetically. These are symmetric bootstrap variants differing only in whether a parametric distribution is assumed.

Direction: regression_sum_of_squares (re) < residual_sum_of_squares (re) — comparing third character: g < s, so source = regression_sum_of_squares. These two partition the total sum of squares and are structurally complementary.

Direction: expected_fisher_information (e) < observed_fisher_information (o) alphabetically. These are the two forms of Fisher information — theoretical expectation vs data-based observed value — forming a symmetric pair.

Added during Agent 6 review (S4-002): credible_interval (Bayesian) and confidence_interval (frequentist) are the canonical dual pair for interval estimation across the two inferential paradigms. Direction: credible (c+r+e) < confidence (c+o) — third char e < o, so source = credible_interval. [Auto-fixed direction: swapped source/target to satisfy alphabetical order convention]

target.canonical_name: Large Sample Confidence Interval using MLE

edges.notes: Design matrix representation as an abstract framework is implemented via dummy encoding for categorical predictors — a standard technique in all regression texts. Confidence raised to 0.97 during Agent 6 review (S4-002).
Added during Agent 6 review: credible_interval is concretely constructed via the equal-tail method. Corrects erroneous instance_of edge deleted from instance_of.json (S3-006).
Added during Agent 6 review: credible_interval is concretely constructed via the HPD interval method. Corrects erroneous instance_of edge deleted from instance_of.json (S3-007).

Added during Agent 6 review (S4-001): MLE objective functions lacking closed-form solutions are routinely maximized via Newton-Raphson. Complements numerical_maximization_methods -> newton_raphson_method by connecting the MLE concept directly to its primary numerical implementation.
Categorical variables are incorporated into regression models via dummy encoding.
Scatterplot is a concrete implementation of data visualization for bivariate relationships.
Scatterplot matrix is a concrete visualization tool for multivariate relationships.
